paragraphs: Cool wind sent a shiver down Sonam’s spine as she ventured into the main city center of Rishikesh. Shops selling colorful clothing and copper statues of India’s gods and restaurants of various vegetarian-cuisine lined both sides of the dirt road. Sonam reached into the pocket of her dress and pulled a tin containing her favorite fruit drops. She gave it a shake and frowned. “One more left. ” She was too distracted to notice Jermaine appear by her side. “Almost out?” Jermaine asked. “Can I see?”

Sonam rolled her eyes at him and pulled back the lid; a lonely blue square waited to be chosen. Jermaine smiled. “How do you go through these so fast?” Sonam looked away and sucked in air. “If you’re going to lecture me on temperance, might I remind you that—” Before she could finish her sentence, the tin rumbled in her hand. She looked down and smiled. The container was filled to the brim with sweet jewels. Sonam’s lips twitched. “Take mine,” Jermaine said. “I don’t like them anyway.” He smiled and raised the tin.“Well, if you’re not going to eat them, then don’t mind if I do!” Sonam said nonchalantly.

Herds of cows wandered through the streets seeking food. Several locals gladly offered food to the cows while others swatted them with metal rods for trying to take fruits and vegetables from their wooden carts.  Jermaine walked around an apple cart and smiled. “Don’t they worship cows?” Sonam rolled her eyes at him and smiled. “I hate to break it to you but not everyone is religious.” As they walked past crowds of men wearing orange clothing, Sonam recalled childhood memories when her grandmother brought her to the city center. Her past and her present collided.  Back then, there were more houses. Today, there were more stores.
“Aren’t those men in orange called babas?” Jermaine asked.

Sonam studied the orange men sitting along a set of steps that led to the blue river. A few of the men were smoking while others sat silently in meditation. “They’re supposed to be very wise!” Jermaine said. “If they were so wise, they’d know that smoking kills,” Sonam spat.  She shrugged and continued walking forward. Then she took note of an abandoned blue building. Before her eyes could focus, Jermaine yelped. “Ouch, Priya! What did you do that for?” Jermaine asked. Priya held her hand out for the boy to see. In the center of her hand, a mosquito twitched in a puddle of blood. Sonam frowned. Her grandmother had taught her a yogic concept called Ahimsa which meant no harm should be done to anyone or to the self. Before the mosquito died, she closed her eyes and said a prayer before rolling her shoulders backwards. “Barbarian.”

“Do you see that? That’s disgusting,” Rashid said, hugging the calf, pointing to a black cow in a corner. It was eating plastic from the ground. Sonam rolled her eyes at her brother. She was too tired to fight with him. Priya scraped the mosquito off her hand on a nearby wall and laughed.

“Indians really are the worst at waste management,” Priya said while stepping over piles of trash. In older times, clay, brass, steel, and copper were used for daily rituals. However, the city of Rishikesh had recently acquired plastic and cardboard.
“Absolutely disgusting!” Sonam said, stepping over a mound of trash.
Suddenly, a skinny man holding a green glass bottle stumbled into the group. He shoved through them and knocked Sonam to the ground. Jermaine sniffed the air as Sonam stood up.
“I thought you can’t buy alcohol in Rishikesh?” Jermaine asked.

“That doesn’t stop people from making it,” Rashid answered. Sonam opened her mouth to counter, but she remembered her vision of Rashid getting married. He was so happy and she was even more proud of him. “You’re right,” Sonam agreed and smiled. Rashid raised a brow and looked at Jermaine. “Watch where you’re going!” Priya screamed in Hindi. “Who do you think you are?” The man, who was undoubtedly intoxicated, turned to Priya and grabbed her by her dress’ collar. “What did you say to me?” he yelled, speech slurring. Before Rashid or Sonam could act, Priya grabbed the drunkard’s wrists and jumped. The bottom of her white sneakers landed on his chest. She used all of her strength to lean back. When her back touched the floor, she hurled the man backward. The villagers gasped as the drunkard landed on a pile of trash. Sonam, fueled by adrenaline, did what she had once seen in a movie: she grabbed a large clay pot from a merchant’s cart and dropped it on the man’s head, rendering him unconscious. Then, turned her head in every direction and saw hungry eyes.
“Come on!” Priya exclaimed. “We need to go before the police show up!”

Sonam, Priya, Rashid, and Jermaine rushed through the crowd with the calf and wandered through alleyways as the sun set. “I think we’re safe,” Sonam said. She’d never broken a pot over a man’s head, nor had she fled the scene of a crime. “We need to go back and apologize,” she suggested. Priya faced her sister and squinted. “You don’t break a clay pot over someone’s head and then apologize! He had it coming! He hurt you!” Priya countered with clenched fists.
“But I didn’t know you had that in you, Sonam,” she added and laughed.
Sonam shared a sly smile. “Did you really think you’re the only one who can fight?”

Rashid studied their surroundings. The end of the alleyway they traveled down led to a large ominous gate. Beyond the rusting bars, a giant building made of stone could be seen through a dark field of overgrown grass and giant trees. The lock on the temple gate was covered with heaps of pollen. A burlap sack filled with seashells leaned against the gate. “What is this place?” Rashid asked. Sonam and Priya approached the gates and studied the rusty lock. Priya used her palm to remove some of the pollen, revealing an engraving. “It’s Sanskrit!” Rashid exclaimed. “But why can’t I read it?” Sonam stepped forward and leaned over. “It’s not your fault. You were never taught ancient Sanskrit.” Rashid rolled his eyes at his sister. “What does it say?” Jermaine asked. Sonam squinted, reciting the words; the blue symbol appeared on her throat but no one noticed. Click! Sonam stepped back when the lock opened and turned to run. “No you don’t!” Priya shouted, gaping. “How did you do that? You better not be practicing witchcraft again! Mom will freak out!”

“Nope. I’m not doing this!” Sonam cried. She tried to run, but Priya grabbed her by her shoulder and held her in place. “You broke a pot over a man’s head!” Priya reminded her sister. Jermaine pushed the gate open and it groaned.
“Should we?” Jermaine asked.
“Absolutely not!” Sonam cried.
“A quick look!” Jermaine pleaded.
“We really need to find Sahil,” Rashid suggested.
Sonam ignored Rashid and rolled her eyes at Jermaine. “Ten minutes. That’s it! If anything goes wrong, I’m running,” Sonam said. “And I’m not coming back for any of you.”
“I’ll make sure to carry a clay pot with me from now on,” Priya muttered, smiling.
“Oh, I’ll drop a house on you!” Sonam smiled. “I am a wicked witch after all.”
Sonam saw Rashid frowning. “Don’t worry. We’ll find that idiot.”

Rashid nodded and Jermaine pushed the gate. “Slowly! We don’t want to wake anyone up! It could be a graveyard!” Priya said. Sonam shuddered. When they walked into the courtyard, a black cat ran past them and hissed at the calf. “This place looks like it hasn’t been visited in years.” Sonam said.

The center of the courtyard had a lonely fountain without water. Sonam walked around it and saw a rusted sword and tangled rope on the soil. Priya passed the fountain and used her forearms to push through curtains of vines that fell from branches overhead. Sonam followed her and saw a large building carved from a single block of black stone basking under silver moonlight. The walls depicted intricate carvings of various people and animals. Priya and Sonam approached the wall. Each pillar on the wall contained a person holding a pot over their head. Large snakes stood next to them.

“Look, Sonam. It’s you!” Jermaine beamed. Sonam used her elbow to jab him. “Very funny!” she smiled. “What is this place?” Rashid asked. “Definitely not a restaurant,” Sonam said and Rashid laughed. She smiled back at him. “Well. What’s the plan now?” Jermaine asked. Rashid and Priya looked at each other. Before either sibling could speak, the calf bolted. Rashid and Jermaine ran after it. Moments later, Priya and Sonam found the three near a set of wide stone steps.
“This must be the entrance of whatever this place is,” Rashid said. “This place looks old.”
“Watch the cow!” Sonam said and ascended the stone steps.
“She breaks a pot over a man’s head and she’s suddenly fearless,” Priya whispered to Jermaine.
“I heard that!” Sonam yelled as she reached large metal doors.

“I don’t like the looks of this!” she added, frowning. The large doors depicted two gigantic serpents; one on each door. They faced each other. Between the serpents, various geometric patterns of petals were carved perfectly into the metal. The doors contained no bolts or latches of any kind. Sonam reached for one of the doors to feel the large snake portrait but Priya snatched her hand. “Shouldn’t you at least read the door?” Sonam exhaled at the ancient sanskrit. “Can you read it?” Priya asked. Sonam nodded. “There’s a warning,” she said, pointing to symbols on the archway of the door. “I’m not sure if my translation is one-hundred percent accurate but it says a mantra is needed to be performed by a god to open the door,” Sonam added. “Wait. There’s more.” Sonam squinted and mouthed words.
“Everything okay?” Jermaine shouted from below. “Need me to come up there?”
Sonam laughed and rolled her eyes. “What an idiot.”
“You like him.”
Sonam faced her sister and cackled. “Him? You’re hilarious.”
Priya smirked. “What else does the door say?”
“It says that all will be judged and the world will be renewed.”
Sonam, unamused by the serpent door, descended the steps. “Nope. It’s time to go.”
“Oh, no you don’t!” Priya said, grabbing her sister’s arm.
“What part of all will be judged do you not understand?” Sonam asked.
Priya squinted at the door and nodded. “Yeah. You’re right.”
“Excuse me, what?” Sonam asked, smiling, eyes twinkling like stars.
“That’s the only time you’ll ever hear me say it!” Priya snapped.

Sonam rolled her eyes at her sister and snickered before retrieving the tin of fruit drops from her pocket. She pulled back the lid, looked away, and offered some to her sister. Priya placed her hand on Sonam’s shoulder and gave her a squeeze. “I forgive you, but you need to apologize to him.” Sonam frowned. “I’m not good at saying sorry or goodbye or I love you or—”
“Or anything that indicates that you have a heart,” Priya smiled. “I’m joking!”
Sonam’s eyes fell. “Maybe you’re right. Maybe there is something wrong with me.”
Priya wrapped her arms around her sister and frowned. “All you have to do is be kind.”
“I’ll try,” Sonam said. “But you know how hard it is for me to tolerate stupidity!”
They smiled and descended the steps with arms linked.
